intro.title: Robotics Vocabulary
intro.tagline: The words you'll meet on day one - defined plainly
intro.body: Robotics conversations are loaded with jargon - frames, poses, costmaps, ROS, IMU, SLAM, MPC, VLA. This page is a fast, opinionated dictionary of the terms you'll see in papers, repos, slack messages, and hardware datasheets. Every entry is one paragraph and points to the branch that goes deeper. Use the search box, or filter by category.

terms.short: A programmable machine that senses, decides, and acts in the physical world.
terms.long: A robot is the closed loop of sensors → compute → actuators. Strip any of those out and you have a tool, a webcam, or a CAD model - not a robot. Industrial arms, Roombas, Spot, and self-driving cars are all robots; smart speakers and chatbots are not (no actuation).

terms.short: An independent way the robot can move - usually one number per joint.
terms.long: A 6-DoF arm has 6 joints, each rotating or sliding independently; a quadcopter has 6 DoF in space (x, y, z, roll, pitch, yaw). DoF is the size of the configuration space and dictates how much trajectory math you'll do.

terms.short: Position + orientation in 3D - usually 6 numbers (x,y,z + roll,pitch,yaw).
terms.long: Pose is the answer to 'where is this thing and which way is it facing?'. In ROS it's geometry_msgs/Pose: a 3D point and a quaternion. End-effector pose, robot pose, object pose - the word is everywhere.

terms.short: A coordinate system attached to a body. ROS tracks the tree of them in tf2.
terms.long: Every meaningful 3D number lives in a frame: 'point at (1, 0, 0.5) in the camera_link frame'. tf2 is the ROS library that maintains the time-varying tree of frames so you can ask 'where is the gripper in the map frame right now?'.

terms.short: Joint angles → end-effector pose. Always solvable.
terms.long: Multiply a chain of homogeneous transforms, one per joint, and out pops the gripper's pose. It's a pure geometry calculation - no optimization, no ambiguity. ROS's KDL or Pinocchio computes it in microseconds.

terms.short: End-effector pose → joint angles. Often hard, can have many solutions or none.
terms.long: The opposite of FK: given a desired hand pose, what joint angles do you set? For redundant arms there are infinitely many answers; for some target poses there are zero. Solvers like TRAC-IK or KDL handle the messy reality.

terms.short: XML file that describes a robot's links + joints to ROS.
terms.long: Unified Robot Description Format. Lists every link, every joint, their parents and children, mass + inertia, and meshes for visualization. xacro is the macro language that keeps URDFs readable. Without a URDF, RViz, MoveIt, and Gazebo can't see your robot.

terms.short: Four numbers that represent a 3D rotation without gimbal lock.
terms.long: (x, y, z, w). Smaller, faster, and singularity-free compared to Euler angles. ROS messages always carry orientation as a quaternion. You almost never compute one by hand - use tf transformations.

terms.short: Roll, pitch, yaw - three rotations stacked. Easy to read, prone to gimbal lock.
terms.long: Roll = around forward axis, pitch = around sideways axis, yaw = around vertical axis. Convenient for humans (drone telemetry, vehicle headings) but breaks down at the poles - use quaternions internally and only convert at the UI.

terms.short: Matrix relating joint velocities to end-effector velocities.
terms.long: If you push joint q at rate q̇, the end-effector moves at J(q)·q̇. Used everywhere: differential IK, force–torque mapping, manipulability analysis, singularity detection. The transpose maps end-effector forces back to joint torques.

terms.short: A configuration where the Jacobian loses rank - robot can't move in some direction.
terms.long: Wrist alignment, fully-extended elbow - the hand is geometrically stuck even though the arm looks fine. Joint speeds blow up near singularities; planners detour around them and controllers cap velocities.

terms.short: The set of poses the end-effector can physically reach.
terms.long: Bounded by joint limits and link lengths. Reachable workspace = positions reachable in some orientation; dexterous workspace = reachable in any orientation. Always sketch this before designing a cell.

terms.short: The business end - gripper, suction cup, welder, scalpel.
terms.long: Whatever's bolted to the last link. Grippers are most common, but suction, magnetic, and tool-changers exist. Pose targets are almost always end-effector pose, not the wrist flange.

terms.short: Two- or multi-finger end-effector that closes around objects.
terms.long: Parallel-jaw is the workhorse. Adaptive (Robotiq), suction (vacuum), and magnetic grippers each fit a specific object class. Force sensing in the fingers turns 'grasp' into 'grasp without crushing'.

terms.short: Moving an end-effector through space to interact with objects.
terms.long: The full stack: kinematics, planning, control, grasping, force feedback. Pick-and-place is the simplest case; deformable manipulation, bimanual, and dexterous in-hand are open research.

terms.short: The thing that moves a joint - usually a motor + gearbox, sometimes pneumatic.
terms.long: Servo (position-controlled), BLDC (high-power), stepper (open-loop), harmonic drive (compact + precise), series-elastic (compliant). The actuator's torque, speed, and stiffness curves bound everything else.

terms.short: Measures how far a joint has rotated. Absolute or incremental.
terms.long: Incremental encoders count ticks; absolute ones report angle directly with no homing. Resolution (counts per revolution) sets your minimum step. Always read encoders before commanding - open-loop motion is where robots break things.

terms.short: Inertial Measurement Unit - accelerometer + gyro, sometimes magnetometer.
terms.long: Tells the robot how it's accelerating and rotating. Drift-prone integration, so always fuse with encoders / GPS / vision. Critical for drones (attitude), legged robots (balance), and mobile robots (heading).

terms.short: Laser scanner - bounces light off the world and times the return.
terms.long: 2D scans for indoor robots, 3D point clouds for AVs and outdoor work. High accuracy, weather-robust, expensive. Sparse compared to images. Pair with cameras for color + geometry.

terms.short: Camera that gives color + depth per pixel - RealSense, Azure Kinect, ZED.
terms.long: Depth from structured light, time-of-flight, or stereo. Indoor friendly, struggles outdoors and on shiny / black surfaces. The starter sensor for most pick-and-place projects.

terms.short: Two cameras spaced apart - depth from disparity.
terms.long: Mimics human binocular vision. Cheap, passive (no IR projector), works outdoors. Calibration-sensitive. Used heavily in autonomous driving (ZED, Stereolabs).

terms.short: Pixel-level brightness-change sensor - microsecond latency, no motion blur.
terms.long: Each pixel asynchronously fires when its brightness changes. Massive temporal resolution, tiny bandwidth, but learning new algorithms. Promising for high-speed drones and sub-mm tracking.

terms.short: 6-axis sensor at the wrist that reports forces and moments.
terms.long: Enables impedance control, contact-rich tasks (peg in hole, polishing), and safe HRI. Integrated into many cobots; expensive as standalone (ATI, Robotiq FT 300).

terms.short: Satellite positioning. Plain GPS is meters-accurate; RTK is centimeter-accurate.
terms.long: RTK uses a fixed base station to subtract atmospheric error from a moving rover. Outdoor AMRs, ag-bots, drones, and autonomous tractors live and die on RTK quality.

terms.short: Discovering the actual numbers (intrinsics, extrinsics) of a sensor or rig.
terms.long: Intrinsic = sensor's internal model (camera focal length); extrinsic = sensor's pose relative to another body (camera-to-base). Hand-eye is the special case of camera-to-end-effector. Bad calibration silently breaks everything downstream.

terms.short: The de-facto middleware + tooling for robot software.
terms.long: Pub-sub topics, request-response services, long-running actions, parameters, launch files, RViz, tf, the colcon build system. ROS 2 (Humble, Jazzy) is what 99% of new projects start on. Learn it; complaining about it does not help your career.

terms.short: A named channel - one publisher writes, many subscribers read.
terms.long: /scan, /odom, /cmd_vel - these are topics. Best for streaming sensor data and commands. Doesn't guarantee delivery; check QoS settings.

terms.short: Synchronous request → response, like an RPC.
terms.long: Use for one-shot config or queries (set parameter, run a check). Don't use for long-running tasks - that's what actions are for.

terms.short: Long-running goal with progress feedback and cancel - like an async service.
terms.long: MoveIt's plan-and-execute, Nav2's NavigateToPose - both actions. Goal → feedback stream → result. Cancellable from client.

terms.short: A process that publishes / subscribes / hosts services on the ROS graph.
terms.long: Roughly one process = one or more nodes. Think 'one well-defined responsibility' per node: a driver, a controller, a planner. Composition lets you fuse nodes into a single process for low-latency.

terms.short: Python or XML script that brings up many nodes with parameters.
terms.long: ROS 2 launch is Python by default. It composes node bringup, sets parameters, includes other launch files, conditionally enables features. The orchestration layer of every robot project.

terms.short: A named config value owned by a node - kp, max_speed, frame_id.
terms.long: Set from launch, YAML, or live (set_parameters_atomically). Lifecycle nodes load parameters once at configure(); plain nodes can take dynamic updates.

terms.short: Quality-of-service profile that controls reliability + history of a topic.
terms.long: Reliable vs best-effort, keep-last vs keep-all, depth, durability, deadlines. The #1 reason your subscriber 'isn't receiving anything' on ROS 2.

terms.short: The transport layer underneath ROS 2 - Fast DDS, Cyclone DDS.
terms.long: Data Distribution Service. Handles discovery, marshaling, multicast, and QoS. You almost never touch it directly, but tuning QoS profiles is tuning DDS.

terms.short: ROS's 3D visualizer - see frames, sensor data, plans live.
terms.long: Plug in TF, point clouds, costmaps, plans, marker arrays, and watch your robot think. Open RViz at the start of any debugging session - it's the cheapest x-ray you have.

terms.short: Modern web-based visualizer + log player for ROS / MCAP.
terms.long: Better timeline scrubbing, multi-panel layouts, and remote viewing than RViz. Pairs with MCAP files, the modern bag format. Free for individuals, paid for teams.

terms.short: Recorded log of ROS messages - replay later for debugging or training.
terms.long: rosbag2 + MCAP store everything published on selected topics with timestamps. Replay is bit-exact. The currency of robotics debugging - collect bags before you fix anything.

terms.short: ROS's manipulation stack - IK, planning, collision-checking, execution.
terms.long: Plug in a URDF + SRDF, get IK, OMPL planners, collision detection, controllers, and a Python/C++ API. Production-grade for arms; battle-tested.

terms.short: ROS's navigation stack - localization + planning + control for mobile robots.
terms.long: Behavior trees, costmaps, planners (Smac, NavFn), controllers (DWB, MPPI, RPP), recovery behaviors. Replaces the old ROS 1 navigation stack and is the default for new mobile work.

terms.short: Simultaneous Localization and Mapping - build a map while figuring out where you are.
terms.long: Online SLAM produces an occupancy grid (or pose graph) the robot can immediately use. Visual SLAM (ORB-SLAM3) for cameras, LiDAR SLAM (Cartographer, slam_toolbox) for laser. Hard problem solved well by libraries.

terms.short: Adaptive Monte-Carlo Localization - particle filter on a known map.
terms.long: Once you have a map, AMCL keeps you localized in it using the LiDAR + odometry. Default localizer in Nav2. Works well in static-ish environments; struggles in dynamic warehouses.

terms.short: 2D map where each cell is free, occupied, or unknown.
terms.long: The bread-and-butter map for indoor mobile robots. Stored as nav_msgs/OccupancyGrid. Costmaps inflate it with safety buffers; planners search over it.

terms.short: Occupancy grid with cell costs - high near obstacles, low in open space.
terms.long: Inflation layer adds a smooth gradient around obstacles so the planner stays away. Static + obstacle + inflation + voxel layers compose into the final cost field. Tuning costmaps is most of Nav2 tuning.

terms.short: Finding a collision-free route from start to goal on a map.
terms.long: A*, Dijkstra, Hybrid-A* on grids; RRT, RRT*, PRM in continuous spaces. Global vs local planners - global plans the route, local makes it executable for the current dynamics.

terms.short: Best-first search with a heuristic - finds shortest path on a grid.
terms.long: f(n) = g(n) + h(n). Optimal when h is admissible. Runs at human speeds on indoor maps; replaced by Hybrid-A* when you also need to plan vehicle orientation.

terms.short: Rapidly-exploring Random Tree - sampling-based planner for high-dim spaces.
terms.long: Grow a tree from start by sampling random points and connecting nearest. RRT* adds rewiring for asymptotic optimality. The default planner family for arms because grids don't scale to 7-DoF.

terms.short: Hierarchical state machine for high-level mission logic.
terms.long: Used in Nav2, MoveIt, and many AVs. Composable, easy to visualize, easy to extend. Replaced finite state machines in modern robotics for non-trivial missions.

terms.short: The 'predict, then update' loop that all probabilistic state estimators inherit from.
terms.long: Every Kalman filter, particle filter, and Bayes-net inference is a special case. Predict pushes belief through a motion model; update multiplies by sensor likelihood and renormalizes.

terms.short: Optimal Bayes filter for linear-Gaussian systems.
terms.long: Predict + update with mean and covariance. Extended Kalman (EKF) linearizes around the current state; Unscented Kalman (UKF) propagates sigma points. The state estimator inside almost every flight controller and IMU+GPS fusion.

terms.short: Bayes filter using a sampled cloud of hypotheses.
terms.long: Each particle is a guess at the state; weights are sensor likelihood. Resample to focus on plausible particles. Handles multi-modal beliefs (kidnapped robot) where Kalman cannot.

terms.short: Feedback controller using proportional + integral + derivative of error.
terms.long: Kp drives current error to zero; Ki kills steady-state error; Kd damps oscillation. Tune in that order. Works on 80% of single-axis problems and is the first thing every roboticist masters.

terms.short: Model Predictive Control - plan a short trajectory, execute the first step, replan.
terms.long: Solve a constrained optimization (over a 1–2 s horizon) every control cycle. Naturally handles constraints + multiple objectives. Used in autonomous driving (lane-keeping), drones, and quadrupeds.

terms.short: Linear Quadratic Regulator - optimal feedback gain for a linear system + quadratic cost.
terms.long: Solve a Riccati equation, get a constant gain matrix K. Stabilizes everything in the basin of linearization. The starting point for cartpole, drone attitude, balancing robots.

terms.short: Make the robot behave like a programmable spring + damper at the end-effector.
terms.long: Instead of commanding stiff position, command a virtual stiffness so contact is gentle. Crucial for assembly, polishing, human-in-the-loop tasks. Admittance is the dual.

terms.short: ROS 2 framework for hardware abstraction + real-time controllers.
terms.long: Hardware interface (talks to motors) + controllers (joint trajectory, diff drive, admittance, etc.) + controller manager. Standard for production robots - write a HW interface, get every standard controller for free.

terms.short: The simplest camera math - a 3D point projected through a tiny hole onto an image plane.
terms.long: Scaled by focal length (fx, fy), shifted by principal point (cx, cy), distorted by lens. The starting model for monocular geometry, calibration, and rectification.

terms.short: Sliding a small kernel over an image - the building block of classical CV and CNNs.
terms.long: Each output pixel = sum of input pixels times kernel weights. Sobel for edges, Gaussian for blur, learned kernels in CNNs. Stride and padding shape the output dimensions.

terms.short: Find every object in an image and label it - bounding box + class.
terms.long: YOLO, DETR, RT-DETR run at 30+ FPS on a Jetson. Train on COCO-style labels. The starting building block for pick-and-place and AV perception.

terms.short: Per-pixel labeling - semantic, instance, or panoptic.
terms.long: Semantic = which class is each pixel; instance = which object instance. SAM and SAM 2 made promptable segmentation a one-liner; great for object isolation in robotics.

terms.short: Estimate the full 3D pose of a known object from an image.
terms.long: Required for grasping known objects. Classical: PnP + textured CAD model. Modern: FoundationPose, MegaPose. Output: object's pose in camera frame, then transform into base.

terms.short: A list of 3D points, often with color or intensity - output of LiDAR or RGB-D.
terms.long: PCL and Open3D are the workhorse libraries. Voxel-downsample, plane-segment, cluster, register, octree. The native data structure for 3D perception.

terms.short: Estimate camera motion frame-to-frame using only images.
terms.long: Step 1 of visual SLAM. Track features, solve essential matrix, integrate. Drifts over time without loop closure. Great for short-term motion estimates on drones and AR headsets.

terms.short: Recognizing a previously-visited place - kills accumulated drift.
terms.long: Without it, SLAM drifts forever. Bag-of-words, NetVLAD, learned descriptors all detect 'we've been here'. Pose-graph optimization corrects the trajectory once a loop closes.

terms.short: Learn a policy from trial-and-error reward signals.
terms.long: Agent + environment + reward. Solve via Q-learning, policy gradients (PPO), actor-critic (SAC). Robotics application: locomotion, dexterous manipulation, simulated driving - usually after a sim-to-real bridge.

terms.short: Learn from demonstrations instead of reward.
terms.long: Behavior cloning, DAgger, diffusion policies. Provide 50–500 teleop episodes, get a working policy. Bypasses the reward-shaping nightmare of RL but inherits the demonstrator's mistakes.

terms.short: Train a policy in simulation, deploy on a real robot.
terms.long: Domain randomization, system identification, action noise injection. Isaac Sim + Isaac Lab, MuJoCo MJX, Genesis, Brax - pick the simulator with the dynamics fidelity you need. Bridges the reality gap.

terms.short: Proximal Policy Optimization - workhorse on-policy RL algorithm.
terms.long: Clipped surrogate objective keeps policy updates small and stable. Default for locomotion (ANYmal, Cassie, humanoids) because it's simple and trains in days.

terms.short: Soft Actor-Critic - sample-efficient off-policy RL with entropy bonus.
terms.long: Replays old experience for sample efficiency, encourages exploration via entropy. Strong on continuous control with limited interaction (real-robot training).

terms.short: Generative behavior cloning - sample actions via a learned diffusion model.
terms.long: Multi-modal demonstrations (multiple correct ways to do a task) collapse the average policy; diffusion handles them by sampling. State-of-the-art for tabletop manipulation in 2024–2026.

terms.short: Vision-Language Model - image + text → text. CLIP, GPT-4V, Gemini, LLaVA.
terms.long: Open-vocabulary scene understanding. 'Is the door open?', 'Where is the red mug?'. Used as zero-shot perception modules in modern robot stacks.

terms.short: Vision-Language-Action model - image + instruction → motor commands.
terms.long: RT-2, OpenVLA, π0, Gemini Robotics. Generalist policies trained on web data + robot demos. The most-watched direction in robot learning right now.

terms.short: Learned dynamics model the agent imagines into instead of querying the real world.
terms.long: Dreamer, DreamerV3, JEPA, V-JEPA. Used for sample-efficient model-based RL and for predicting consequences of actions a few steps ahead.

terms.short: AGV follows fixed routes; AMR navigates autonomously around obstacles.
terms.long: AGV = magnetic-tape or wire-guided forklift; cheap, deterministic, brittle to layout changes. AMR = SLAM + planning + dynamic obstacle avoidance; expensive, flexible, the modern default in fulfillment.

terms.short: Open protocol for vendor-neutral AMR fleet control.
terms.long: JSON-over-MQTT messages between fleet manager and individual AMRs: orders, state, factsheets, instant actions. Adopted by VW, BMW, Bosch, and most fleet-management vendors.

terms.short: Multi-Agent Path Finding - plan paths for many robots without collisions.
terms.long: Conflict-Based Search (CBS), Priority-Based Search (PBS), windowed variants. The core algorithm for warehouse fleets and orchestrated drone shows.

terms.short: 0 (manual) → 5 (full autonomy) classification for self-driving cars.
terms.long: L2 = ADAS (Tesla AP, GM Super Cruise). L3 = conditional autonomy with handover (Mercedes Drive Pilot). L4 = no human in the loop within a domain (Waymo, Cruise). L5 doesn't exist yet.

terms.short: Functional safety standard for road vehicles - ASIL A through D.
terms.long: Hazard analysis, risk assessment, ASIL decomposition, hardware-in-the-loop. Required for any production AV or ADAS feature. Pairs with SOTIF (ISO 21448) for ML-driven functions.

terms.short: Industrial robot + collaborative robot safety standards.
terms.long: ISO 10218 = the bones of industrial robot safety (cells, fences, e-stops). TS 15066 = the cobot extension that allows controlled contact with humans. Required reading for HRI and cell design.

terms.short: OS configuration that bounds worst-case latency - needed for low-level control.
terms.long: PREEMPT_RT patch + cyclictest verification. Without it, your 1 kHz controller will jitter under load. Required for force control and any servo loop above ~200 Hz.

terms.short: NVIDIA's embedded GPU compute family for robots - Orin, Nano, NX.
terms.long: x86-class compute in a 25-60W package with CUDA. The default brain for vision-heavy mobile robots in 2026. Pair with Isaac ROS for accelerated perception.

terms.short: Open-source flight stack for drones - multirotor, fixed-wing, VTOL.
terms.long: Ardupilot's modern cousin: better simulation, MAVLink, ROS 2 bridge, lots of vendor adoption. Pair with QGroundControl + Gazebo for the standard hobby-to-pro path.

terms.short: Lightweight binary protocol drones use for telemetry and commanding.
terms.long: Pub-sub-ish over UART or UDP. Heartbeat, attitude, GPS, mission, command_long. Spoken by every serious autopilot. ROS 2 bridges via mavros.

terms.short: Collaborative robot - designed to work near humans without fences.
terms.long: Force-limited joints, rounded geometry, safety-rated software (UR, Franka, Kuka iiwa). Lower payload than industrial arms in exchange for safety. Governed by ISO/TS 15066.

terms.short: A human directly controls a remote robot, often through VR or a leader-follower rig.
terms.long: Enables data collection (for imitation learning), remote ops, surgery (da Vinci). Latency budget < 100ms for natural feel. ALOHA, GELLO, Mobile ALOHA are the current research rigs.

terms.short: The core control cycle every robot runs: sense, decide, actuate, repeat.
terms.long: Sensors stream measurements; perception interprets them; planning chooses an action; control issues commands; the world changes; sensors stream again. Robotics is the engineering of this loop closing fast and reliably.

terms.short: Closed-loop uses feedback; open-loop just commands and hopes.
terms.long: Open-loop ('go forward 2 seconds') is brittle - any disturbance and you're off target. Closed-loop ('drive until odometry says 2 meters') corrects in real time using sensor feedback. 99% of robot motion is closed-loop for this reason.

terms.short: Estimating motion by integrating wheel speeds, IMU, or visual cues.
terms.long: Drifts with time, but cheap and always available. Combine with absolute references (GPS, AMCL on a map) to bound the drift. The /odom topic is the bread-and-butter of mobile-robot localization.

terms.short: The full hierarchy of frames in a ROS robot: map → odom → base_link → sensors.
terms.long: Every frame has a parent; tf2 publishes the time-varying transforms between them. `ros2 run tf2_tools view_frames` draws the tree as a PDF. Broken TF tree = broken robot.

terms.short: The space of all possible joint settings. A 7-DoF arm has a 7D C-space.
terms.long: Planning happens in C-space, not workspace. A point in C-space is a full robot pose; obstacles in workspace become forbidden regions in C-space. Sampling-based planners (RRT) randomly sample this high-dim space.

terms.short: Choosing where and how to close fingers around an object.
terms.long: Antipodal pairs, force closure, learned grasp scores (GraspNet, AnyGrasp). Picks one stable grasp from many candidates, considering reachability and clutter.

terms.short: Open Motion Planning Library - the OMPL inside MoveIt.
terms.long: RRT, RRT*, PRM, KPIECE, and dozens of others, all behind one C++ API. MoveIt picks the planner; you usually just enable / disable specific algorithms in YAML.

terms.short: Per-pixel motion vectors between two consecutive images.
terms.long: Lucas-Kanade (sparse), Farnebäck (dense), modern learned flow (RAFT). Used in visual odometry, drone hover, action recognition. Sensitive to lighting and texture.

terms.short: Per-pixel distance from camera. Bigger disparity = closer.
terms.long: Stereo matches left and right images to compute disparity, then converts to metric depth via the baseline + focal length. RGB-D cameras give it directly. Holes are the common failure mode.

terms.short: Graph where nodes are robot poses and edges are relative measurements.
terms.long: Modern SLAM is pose-graph optimization: minimize the sum of squared edge errors using g2o, GTSAM, or Ceres. A loop closure adds a long-range edge that tightens everything.

terms.short: Compact image descriptors used for place recognition + loop closure.
terms.long: Reduce an image to a fingerprint vector. Compare new fingerprints against the database; hits indicate the robot has been here. NetVLAD replaced classical BoW for most modern stacks.

terms.short: Imitation learning by supervised regression of demo (state → action) pairs.
terms.long: Simplest IL approach. Works on cleanly-collected demos but compounding errors push the policy off-distribution. DAgger and diffusion policies fix the worst cases.

terms.short: Adding small auxiliary rewards to make sparse-reward RL tractable.
terms.long: If the only reward is at task completion, RL may never see one. Shaping rewards (closer-to-goal, energy penalties) gives gradient. Done carelessly it breeds reward hacking.

terms.short: Model-based learns a dynamics model and plans through it; model-free learns a policy directly.
terms.long: Model-based (Dreamer, MuZero, PETS) is sample-efficient but compounds modeling error. Model-free (PPO, SAC) is simpler and dominant in practice. World models are reviving model-based methods.

terms.short: Big pretrained model (LLM, VLM, VLA) reused across many downstream tasks.
terms.long: Train once on internet-scale data, fine-tune or prompt for specific tasks. The frontier of robot policies is foundation-model-based: VLAs (RT-2, OpenVLA, π0), perception (DINOv2, SAM 2), planning (GPT-4o, Gemini).

terms.short: Closed-loop motion control - keep tracking a target until the error is zero.
terms.long: Visual servoing tracks an image feature; force servoing tracks a contact force; position servoing tracks a setpoint. The verb 'to servo' = 'to control toward'.

terms.short: The target value the controller is trying to achieve.
terms.long: For a position loop, the desired position. For a velocity loop, desired velocity. The error = setpoint - measurement, fed into PID / MPC / etc.

terms.short: Long-term offset between setpoint and measurement after the controller settles.
terms.long: P-only controllers leave residual error; the I term integrates it away. Tuning hint: too much I = oscillation, too little = lazy convergence.

terms.short: Adding a model-based command on top of feedback for faster tracking.
terms.long: If you know the desired acceleration, just send the corresponding torque - don't wait for error to build up. Feedforward + feedback is the recipe for high-performance trajectory tracking.

terms.short: A controller is stable if errors stay bounded; unstable if they grow.
terms.long: Linear stability is decided by the closed-loop poles being in the left half-plane. Nonlinear stability uses Lyapunov functions. The first thing every control engineer checks.

terms.short: Hardware-enforced stop that brings the robot to a halt within a guaranteed time.
terms.long: Cat 0 (immediate power cut), Cat 1 (controlled stop, then power cut), Cat 2 (controlled stop, power on). Required by ISO 10218 in any robot cell with humans nearby.

terms.short: Physical Intelligence's open-source flow-matching VLAs - the 2026 reference policy.
terms.long: π0 (Oct 2024) introduced flow-matching action heads for smooth high-frequency continuous control; π0.5 (Apr 2025) added open-world generalization, cleaning entirely unseen kitchens. Weights and code are public and the model is the most replicated VLA baseline in 2026.

terms.short: NVIDIA's 2B humanoid VLA with a System 2 (VLM) + System 1 (motor) split.
terms.long: Released March 2025. Trained on Cosmos-synthesized video plus real humanoid datasets. The dual-system arch separates slow VLM reasoning from fast motor control. Vendor-neutral - Figure, Apptronik, 1X, Unitree all run derivatives.

terms.short: Figure AI's in-house VLA powering Figure 02 / 03 humanoids on the BMW line.
terms.long: Closed-source. Two-system architecture similar to GR00T. Deployed in production at BMW with 1,250+ shift-hours and 90,000+ parts handled. The first VLA running real production work for OEM revenue.

terms.short: Optimized Fine-Tuning extension for OpenVLA - 25-50x inference speedup, bimanual control.
terms.long: Drop-in replacement for OpenVLA fine-tuning that fixes the speed and dexterity gaps. Lets you fine-tune the 7B base model for high-frequency continuous control with multiple input cameras.

terms.short: Continuous-action sampler used by π0 and π0.5 - smoother and faster than diffusion at inference.
terms.long: Learns a velocity field that pushes Gaussian noise to the data distribution along straight paths. Fewer integration steps than DDPM diffusion, naturally outputs continuous actions, no token discretization. Standard for high-frequency robot policies in 2026.

terms.short: World foundation model family (7B / 14B) trained on 20M hours of real-world video.
terms.long: Released Jan 2025. 9,000T training tokens. Used for synthesizing photoreal training data for AVs / humanoids and (via Cosmos Policy) as an action-conditioned planner. Downloaded over 2M times by Jan 2026.

terms.short: Meta's feature-space world model - 80% zero-shot pick-and-place after only 62h of robot data.
terms.long: Pretrained on 1M+ hours of internet video as a Joint Embedding Predictive Architecture (no pixel reconstruction). Action conditioning added with tiny robot data, transfers zero-shot across labs and arm types. Yann LeCun's bet on representation-space prediction over pixel prediction.

terms.short: DeepMind's ~11B real-time interactive world model - 24 fps persistent 3D environments.
terms.long: Generates explorable 3D worlds from a text prompt with persistent state across long horizons. Used as a sim source for embodied AI training. Pixel-space generative (the opposite philosophical bet from V-JEPA).

terms.short: 2024 result showing 26K demos x 5 tasks + Diffusion Policy beats algorithmic tinkering.
terms.long: Stanford / DeepMind. Used the open ALOHA 2 hardware at scale to train hard bimanual tasks (deformable objects, contact-rich assembly). The 'just scale demos' moment for imitation learning.

terms.short: HuggingFace's library + dataset hub for robot learning - the 2026 default starting point.
terms.long: One package for ACT, Diffusion Policy, π0, ALOHA datasets, Open-X-Embodiment, DROID, training, eval. Dataset format is the de facto exchange format for new robot data releases.

terms.short: 1M+ trajectory cross-embodiment dataset spanning 22 robots from 60 datasets and 34 labs.
terms.long: The training-data foundation for OpenVLA, Octo, RT-X, GR00T, and most modern VLAs. Standardized format. Mirrored on LeRobot Hub.

terms.short: Tesla's humanoid robot in mass production at Fremont since Jan 2026.
terms.long: 22 DoF per hand with a tendon-driven biomimetic actuation system, 50 total actuators per arm. Targeted at factory and home tasks. Tesla data-collection ops run a wide teleop fleet to generate training data.

terms.short: Figure AI's commercial humanoid - deployed at BMW running the in-house Helix VLA.
terms.long: Figure 03's BotQ factory ramps to 12,000 units/year. 02 supported 30,000+ vehicles, 1,250+ shift-hours, 90,000+ parts handled in real production pilots. The first humanoid earning OEM revenue.

terms.short: Industrial humanoid robot deployed at Mercedes-Benz; $5B valuation in Feb 2026.
terms.long: Heavy-duty payload focus, electric actuators, tethered + untethered modes. $520M raise in Feb 2026 specifically targeting Tesla / Chinese humanoid competition.

terms.short: 1X's consumer humanoid - $20K outright or $499/month, deliveries 2026.
terms.long: First humanoid actually shipping to homes (vs labs / factories). Soft-bodied design for HRI safety. Pre-orders opened October 2025; deliveries begin 2026. 1X collects all teleop training data via an in-house body suit.

terms.short: Boston Dynamics' all-electric humanoid - shipping to Hyundai's Georgia plant.
terms.long: Replaces the famous hydraulic Atlas. More joints, quieter, longer endurance, full electric. First customer is Hyundai's Metaplant for real factory work.

terms.short: Lower-cost humanoids out of China; H1 is the heavy variant, G1 the agile lightweight.
terms.long: Major competitor on price-per-DoF. G1 starts well under $20K. Standard ROS 2 / SDK support. Used heavily as research platform outside China and as production deployments inside.

terms.short: Baidu's robotaxi service - 1,000+ driverless vehicles in 15 cities, 11M+ rides.
terms.long: Operates in mainland China plus Dubai and Abu Dhabi as of 2025-2026. Built on the Apollo open-source AV stack. The largest commercial L4 robotaxi deployment by ride volume.

terms.short: ROS 2-native open AV stack; 2026 end-to-end diffusion-based version piloting at 50 sites in Japan.
terms.long: Maintained by the Autoware Foundation (Tier IV-led). Modular ROS 2 stack from perception to control. Drop-in for any ROS 2 vehicle. The most-adopted open AV stack in academia.

terms.short: NVIDIA's high-performance GPU sim framework on top of Isaac Sim 5.1 / 6.0 - the 2026 default for humanoid + arm RL.
terms.long: Photoreal, parallel, supports thousands of envs on a single GPU. Tight integration with Isaac Sim, Cosmos, and the GR00T training pipeline. ROS 2 Jazzy bridge built in.
